title: Amorphous vs. Short-Range-Ordered Complexions: Consequences for Grain-Boundary-Mediated Plasticity in Nanocrystalline Al-Ni Alloys
abstract: Amorphous grain-boundary (GB) complexions in thermally stable nanocrystalline alloys are commonly assumed to be structurally homogeneous, yet their disordered nature makes them susceptible to local short-range ordering (SRO). The influence of local SRO on GB-mediated plasticity mechanisms in such complexions remains poorly understood. This article employs large-scale Monte Carlo and molecular dynamics simulation to address this gap through simulations of nanocrystalline Al-Ni alloys at two Ni concentrations, 2 at.% and 4 at.%. Annealing at 913 K produces thick uniform amorphous intergranular film complexions, while annealing at 378 K produces semi-amorphous complexions containing FCC-type and BCC-type SRO. These two complexion states produce fundamentally different mechanical responses. Amorphous complexions act as dislocation sinks, suppressing shear localization and promoting homogeneous plasticity through shear transformation zones, but at the cost of lower strength. SRO complexions generate higher strength but also promote heterogeneous stress concentrations across the GB network, leading to intense shear localization regardless of Ni concentration. This contrast reflects a fundamental shift in governing mechanism, from shear-transformation-zone-controlled behavior in amorphous complexion alloys to GB-stress-heterogeneity-controlled behavior in SRO complexion alloys. These findings highlight the potential of complexion engineering to tailor the mechanical properties of nanocrystalline materials.
summary: This paper explores how the structural state of grain boundaries—either purely amorphous or containing short-range order—dictates the mechanical properties of nanocrystalline Al-Ni alloys. It demonstrates that while SRO increases material strength, it also triggers detrimental shear localization. These findings suggest that controlling the structural order at interfaces can be used to engineer the plasticity of nanostructured materials.
main_problem: The study investigates how local short-range ordering (SRO) within amorphous grain-boundary complexions influences the plasticity and mechanical response of nanocrystalline Al-Ni alloys.
main_result: Amorphous grain boundaries promote homogeneous plasticity via shear transformation zones but lower strength, whereas SRO-containing boundaries increase strength but induce intense shear localization due to stress heterogeneity.
method: Large-scale hybrid Monte Carlo and Molecular Dynamics (MC/MD) simulations using the LAMMPS framework and an EAM interatomic potential.
model_or_system: Nanocrystalline Al-Ni alloys (Al-2Ni and Al-4Ni) consisting of an FCC Al polycrystal with approximately 13 million atoms and an average grain size of 24 nm.
key_observables: Stress-strain curves, grain-boundary thickness, radial distribution functions (RDF), polyhedral template matching (PTM) for structural ordering, and atomic-scale von Mises stress and strain distributions.
important_parameters: Ni concentrations (2 at.% and 4 at.%), annealing temperatures (378 K and 913 K), and engineering strain rate (10^8 s^-1).
main_assumptions: The interatomic interactions are accurately captured by the EAM potential, and the grain boundary complexions reach equilibrium via the simulated annealing regimes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the equilibrium microstructure and Ni segregation; Figure 2 presents the Al-Ni phase diagram; Figure 3 compares Ni-Ni RDF curves; Figure 4 visualizes the structural difference between amorphous and SRO complexions; Figures 5-6 show tensile behavior and shear strain; Figures 7-8 compare atomic-scale von Mises stress distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of GB-mediated plasticity, describes the MC/MD simulation setup and structural analysis tools, presents the structural evolution of GBs under different annealing temperatures, compares the mechanical response (strength vs. localization) of amorphous and SRO regimes, and concludes with implications for complexion engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Equilibrium microstructure and Ni solute segregation in the Al-4Ni polycrystal predicted by MC/MD simulation at 378 K and then cooled to 300 K. Each grain is highlighted in different colors according to their crystallographic orientation.
Figure 2: Equilibrium Al-Ni phase diagram for total Ni concentrations up to 4 at.% predicted by MC/MD.

Figure 3: Radial distribution function curves for Ni-Ni pairs at grain boundaries in the nanocrystalline Al-4Ni alloy annealed at 913 K and 378 K. The first four nearest-neighbor (NN) distances in a perfect FCC Ni unit cell are indicated by dashed lines.

Figure 4. Close-up view of the same grain-boundary region in nanocrystalline Al-4Ni annealed at 913 K and 378 K, revealing amorphous and short-range-ordered (SRO) Ni complexions, respectively. Structural analysis shown with (a) all atoms and (b) Ni solute atoms only.

Figure 5. Effect of amorphous complexions on deformation in nanocrystalline Al-Ni alloys (annealed at 913 K): (a) simulated tensile stress-strain curves and (b) atomic-scale snapshots of local von Mises shear strain at 300 K.
